course_info.title: Video Production and Editing
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Map the complete video production pipeline from concept to distribution
Identify the roles and responsibilities in a professional video production team
Evaluate production scope and create realistic timelines for video projects

lecture_notes.introduction: This course elevates your video production skills from social media clips to professional-grade content. You will learn to plan, shoot, and edit video projects that meet broadcast and corporate production standards using professional tools and workflows.
key_concepts: Three-phase production: pre-production (planning), production (shooting), post-production (editing)
Pre-production deliverables: script, storyboard, shot list, location scout, talent casting, equipment plan
Production roles: director, DP/camera operator, sound recordist, gaffer, production assistant
Post-production workflow: media management, assembly, rough cut, fine cut, color, sound, export
Project scoping: estimating time, resources, and budget for different video formats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Behind-the-scenes breakdown of a professional corporate video production, tracing every deliverable from initial brief through final delivery, with time allocation for each phase
setup_requirements: Adobe Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve installed
Camera checkout from equipment room (DSLR or mirrorless)
External audio recorder and lavalier microphone

review.value: Professional productions typically spend 60-70% of total project time in pre-production; thorough planning prevents expensive reshoots and editing problems
The systematic process of ingesting, organizing, backing up, and cataloging footage using consistent naming conventions and folder structures before editing begins

learning_objectives: Control exposure manually using ISO, aperture, and shutter speed
Apply cinematic composition and framing techniques
Execute basic camera movements: pan, tilt, dolly, and tracking shots
readings: Brown, Cinematography: Theory and Practice, Ch. 1-4
No Film School, 'Camera Basics for Video Production'

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding your camera as a creative tool rather than an automatic recording device is what separates professional video from amateur footage. Manual control of exposure and deliberate composition create the visual quality your audience expects.
key_concepts: Exposure triangle: ISO (sensor sensitivity), aperture (depth of field), shutter speed (motion blur)
White balance: matching color temperature to lighting conditions for accurate color
Composition rules: rule of thirds, leading lines, depth layers (foreground/middle/background)
Shot types: wide/establishing, medium, close-up, extreme close-up, over-the-shoulder
Camera movements: pan (horizontal rotation), tilt (vertical rotation), dolly (forward/back), tracking (side-to-side)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on camera exercise shooting the same scene in full auto vs. manual mode, comparing results, then practicing each shot type and camera movement with instructor feedback
setup_requirements: DSLR or mirrorless camera with manual controls

review.value: ISO, aperture, and shutter speed form an interconnected system where changing one requires adjusting another to maintain proper exposure; creative choices in each parameter also affect image characteristics (noise, depth of field, motion blur)
For natural-looking motion blur in video, set shutter speed to double the frame rate (e.g., 1/50 at 24fps); faster shutter speeds create a staccato look, slower speeds create excessive blur

learning_objectives: Set up three-point lighting for interview and presentation scenarios
Modify lighting quality using diffusion, reflection, and flags
Light a scene for mood and narrative purpose beyond basic exposure
readings: Brown, Cinematography: Theory and Practice, Ch. 7-9
Film Riot, 'Lighting Basics for Filmmakers' (video tutorial series)

lecture_notes.introduction: Light is the raw material of video. Learning to see light, shape it, and use it intentionally transforms your video production from flat documentation to cinematic storytelling. Even with budget constraints, understanding lighting principles dramatically improves production value.
key_concepts: Three-point lighting: key light (primary), fill light (shadow reduction), back light (subject separation)
Hard vs. soft light: direct sources create hard shadows; diffused sources create soft, flattering light
Color temperature: measuring light warmth/coolness in Kelvin; mixing temperatures intentionally or avoiding it
Practical lighting: using existing light sources within the scene as motivated lighting
Lighting for mood: high-key (bright, even, optimistic), low-key (dramatic, shadowed, mysterious)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a three-point interview lighting setup from scratch, then modifying it for three different moods: corporate professional, dramatic documentary, and warm lifestyle content
setup_requirements: LED panel lights (2-3 available from equipment room)

review.value: Key light provides primary illumination from 30-45 degrees off-axis, fill light reduces shadows on the opposite side at lower intensity, back light separates the subject from the background by creating an edge highlight
Material placed between the light source and the subject that scatters light rays, converting a hard point source into a larger, softer source that wraps around the subject and reduces harsh shadows

learning_objectives: Select and deploy appropriate microphone types for different video scenarios
Record clean audio in the field using professional techniques
Synchronize dual-system audio with video in post-production
readings: Curtis Judd, 'Audio for Video Creators' (YouTube series, episodes 1-5)

lecture_notes.introduction: Audiences will tolerate imperfect video but will not tolerate bad audio. The fastest way to make any video feel amateur is to have poor sound quality. Professional audio recording in the field and in the studio is an essential video production skill.
key_concepts: Microphone types for video: shotgun (on-camera directional), lavalier (clip-on for interviews), boom (overhead directional)
Recording environments: controlling room acoustics, minimizing ambient noise, using windscreens outdoors
Dual-system recording: using an external audio recorder for higher quality, syncing with video in post
Audio levels: monitoring with headphones during recording, targeting -12dB to -6dB peaks
Room tone: recording 30 seconds of ambient silence at each location for seamless audio editing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Recording an interview setup with three microphone configurations (on-camera shotgun, lavalier, boom), comparing audio quality, then demonstrating dual-system sync in Premiere Pro
setup_requirements: External audio recorder (Zoom H4n or equivalent)

review.value: A 30-second recording of the ambient sound in a shooting location, captured with the same microphone setup used for dialogue; essential for filling gaps in audio edits without jarring silence
Recording audio on a separate device from the camera for higher quality and more control, then synchronizing in post-production using timecode, slate clap, or automatic waveform matching

class_schedule.topic: Non-Linear Editing: Assembly to Fine Cut
learning_objectives: Organize a professional editing project with bins, sequences, and media management
Execute the editing workflow from assembly cut through rough cut to fine cut
Apply continuity editing principles: match cuts, L-cuts, J-cuts, and cutaways
readings: Murch, In the Blink of an Eye, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Editing is where the story is truly constructed. The shooting gives you raw material; the editing gives you the story. Professional editing is methodical: you progress through defined stages, each refining the story further until the final cut emerges.
key_concepts: Project organization: bin structure, naming conventions, proxy workflows for large projects
Assembly cut: placing all usable footage in story order without timing refinement
Rough cut: establishing pacing, removing weak material, identifying missing elements
Fine cut: frame-accurate trimming, transition refinement, audio smoothing
Continuity editing: match on action, eyeline match, L-cuts (audio leads video), J-cuts (video leads audio)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Editing a 2-minute interview segment from raw footage: importing and organizing, building the assembly, refining to rough cut with B-roll, and polishing to fine cut with L-cuts and audio transitions
setup_requirements: Adobe Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve
Sample interview footage with B-roll (provided on LMS)
External hard drive or fast storage for media

review.value: L-cut: audio from the current clip continues while the video cuts to the next shot, creating smooth transitions. J-cut: audio from the next clip begins before its video appears, building anticipation. Both make edits feel invisible.

A progressive refinement process: assembly (all footage in order), rough cut (pacing established, weak material removed), fine cut (frame-accurate edits, polished transitions), preventing premature detail work on sections that may be cut

learning_objectives: Perform primary color correction to normalize footage for consistent exposure and white balance
Apply secondary color grading to create a visual mood or style
Use scopes (waveform, vectorscope, histogram) to evaluate and adjust color objectively
readings: van Hurkman, Color Correction Handbook, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Color grading is the process that gives professional video its distinctive look. It separates social media video from cinematic content. Understanding color correction (making it right) and color grading (making it feel right) are essential post-production skills.
key_concepts: Primary correction: normalizing exposure, white balance, and contrast to create a consistent baseline
Secondary grading: targeted adjustments to specific colors, luminance ranges, or image regions
Color scopes: waveform (luminance), vectorscope (color saturation/hue), histogram (exposure distribution)
LUTs (Look-Up Tables): pre-built color transformations for quick stylistic starting points
Matching shots: ensuring consistent color across different cameras, locations, and lighting conditions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Color correcting a multi-camera interview: normalizing all angles to match, then applying a consistent creative grade, using scopes to verify technical accuracy throughout
setup_requirements: DaVinci Resolve or Adobe Premiere Pro Lumetri panel
Color calibrated monitor (recommended) or reference images for comparison

review.value: Primary correction adjusts the entire image globally (exposure, white balance, contrast); secondary grading targets specific elements (make the sky bluer, warm skin tones) for creative and corrective purposes
A scope showing luminance values from 0 (black) to 100 (white) across the image frame; used to ensure proper exposure, identify clipping, and maintain broadcast-safe levels

questions.question: Explain the exposure triangle and describe how you would adjust settings to achieve a shallow depth of field in a well-lit outdoor scene while maintaining proper exposure.
questions.answer: The exposure triangle consists of ISO (sensor sensitivity), aperture (size of the lens opening), and shutter speed (duration of exposure). For shallow depth of field, use a wide aperture (low f-number like f/2.8). In a well-lit outdoor scene, this would overexpose the image, so compensate by: (1) lowering ISO to the base value (100), and (2) increasing shutter speed to reduce light reaching the sensor. If the scene is too bright even at base ISO and maximum shutter speed, use a Neutral Density (ND) filter to reduce light without changing aperture.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all three exposure elements
Identifies wide aperture for shallow DOF
Explains compensation techniques for bright conditions

questions.question: Describe the three-point lighting setup and explain why recording room tone is essential for video editing.
questions.answer: Three-point lighting uses: (1) Key light - the primary, brightest source placed 30-45 degrees off-axis from the camera, establishing the main illumination direction. (2) Fill light - a softer, dimmer source on the opposite side that reduces shadow intensity without eliminating shadows. (3) Back light (or hair light) - placed behind the subject to create a rim of light separating them from the background. Room tone is essential because every location has unique ambient sound. When editing dialogue, removing a word or cutting between takes creates gaps that would be dead silent, which sounds unnatural and jarring. Room tone fills these gaps with the location's ambient sound, making edits seamless and invisible.

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three lights and their roles
Explains why room tone matters for editing

questions.question: Explain the difference between an L-cut and a J-cut, and describe a scenario where each would be the appropriate editing choice.
questions.answer: In an L-cut, the audio from the current clip continues playing while the video cuts to the next shot. Example: an interviewee finishes a statement about their childhood home, and while their audio continues, the video cuts to old photographs. The audio bridges the visual transition smoothly. In a J-cut, the audio from the next clip begins before its video appears. Example: before cutting to a bustling city street, the sounds of traffic and crowds begin over the previous shot, building anticipation for the visual transition. L-cuts are best for letting a speaker's words carry over illustrative visuals. J-cuts are best for building anticipation or establishing context before a scene change.

questions.question: What is the difference between primary color correction and secondary color grading? Why must primary correction be completed before creative grading begins?
questions.answer: Primary color correction adjusts the entire image globally: fixing exposure, correcting white balance, and normalizing contrast to create a technically accurate baseline. Secondary color grading targets specific elements (hue ranges, luminance zones, or masked regions) to create a creative visual style. Primary correction must come first because creative grading amplifies the characteristics of the underlying footage. If the footage has inconsistent exposure or incorrect white balance, creative grading will amplify those problems. Starting from a normalized baseline ensures the creative grade applies consistently across all shots and cameras.

rubric.key: Explains why primary must precede secondary

major_assignments.title: Professional Short Documentary
major_assignments.description: Produce a 3-5 minute short documentary on a local story, person, or organization. The project must demonstrate professional pre-production planning, cinematography, lighting, audio recording, and post-production editing including color grading. The documentary must tell a complete story with a clear narrative arc.
objectives: Execute the full production pipeline from pre-production planning through final delivery
Demonstrate professional camera operation, lighting, and audio recording
Edit a compelling narrative using continuity editing techniques
Apply color grading to create a consistent visual style
deliverables: Pre-production package: script/outline, shot list, storyboard, production schedule
Final documentary (3-5 minutes) exported at 1080p or 4K
Behind-the-scenes documentation: photos and notes from production
Technical report (2-3 pages) documenting equipment, settings, and post-production decisions

criteria: Script/outline establishes a clear narrative structure
Shot list is comprehensive and demonstrates planning for visual storytelling
Production schedule is realistic and accounts for setup time
Storyboard communicates visual intentions effectively
Camera work demonstrates manual exposure control and intentional composition
Lighting is appropriate and contributes to the visual story
Audio is clean, properly leveled, and recorded with appropriate microphones
Multiple shot types and camera angles create visual variety
Story is coherent with a clear beginning, middle, and end
Editing rhythm supports the narrative pacing
L-cuts, J-cuts, and cutaways are used effectively
Color grading is consistent and enhances the visual story
Equipment and settings are documented accurately
Post-production decisions are explained with reasoning
Challenges and solutions are discussed honestly
Documentation demonstrates understanding of technical concepts
Professional pre-production demonstrating thorough planning
High-quality cinematography, lighting, and audio in production
Compelling editorial storytelling with polished post-production
Thorough technical documentation reflecting deep understanding

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on production techniques and post-production concepts

lab_exercises.description: Weekly hands-on exercises in camera, lighting, audio, and editing

major_assignment.description: Professional short documentary demonstrating full production pipeline

midterm_production.description: Mid-semester production showcasing camera, lighting, and audio skills

final_portfolio.description: Final reel and portfolio demonstrating growth across all production skills

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and production days. Equipment is shared and schedules are coordinated; absence affects the entire team. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a full letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Production exercises with equipment checkout deadlines cannot be extended. Final projects submitted late receive maximum 50% credit.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises involve crew roles and teamwork. All graded projects are individual or assigned-team submissions. Sharing raw footage, project files, or completed edits between non-team-members is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All footage must be original. Music and stock footage must be properly licensed. Documentary subjects must consent to being filmed. Fabricating documentary content or staging scenes without disclosure is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Adobe Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve installed on their laptop, headphones, and an external hard drive (minimum 500GB). Cameras, lighting, and audio equipment are available for checkout from the equipment room. Personal cameras may be used if they support manual controls.
